description:
A MySQL slow–query log has been placed in your workspace under
      /home/user/db_logs/slow_query.log  

    You are acting as a database administrator who has been asked to
    1) summarise the overall latency of the captured queries, and  
    2) isolate any individual statements whose run-time is over five full
       seconds.

    Requirements
    -------------
    A. Generate the summary file /home/user/db_logs/query_metrics.log  
       • The file must contain exactly three **newline-separated** lines  
         in the exact order shown below (no blank lines, no extra
         spaces):  

           Average_Query_Time(s)=<avg>  
           Longest_Query_Time(s)=<max>  
           Total_Queries=<count>  

       • <avg> is the arithmetic mean of every “Query_time” value that
         appears in the log, rounded to **three decimal places** using
         normal rounding rules.  
       • <max> is the highest “Query_time” value found in the log,
         expressed with exactly **three digits after the decimal point**
         (pad with trailing zeros if necessary).  
       • <count> is the total number of “Query_time” entries found.  

       The automated tests will do a literal string comparison, so make
       sure the file contains nothing except those three lines.

    B. Create /home/user/db_logs/high_latency_queries.sql  
       • Scan the same log file and extract the full SQL statement line
         (the line that ends in a semicolon) for every query whose
         “Query_time” is **strictly greater than 5** seconds.  
       • Write those statement lines, in the original order they appear
         in the log, each on its own line, preserving all spacing,
         punctuation and the trailing semicolon.  
       • Do not include any other text (headers, timestamps, blank
         lines, etc.).

    Constraints
    -----------
    • You may use any combination of awk, sed, sort, head, tail, cut,
      etc., but do not rely on utilities that are unlikely to be present
      in a minimal Linux environment.  
    • Do not elevate privileges; everything must be done as the normal
      user.  
    • The directory /home/user/db_logs already exists and is writable.  
    • Only the two files named above should be created/modified.

    When you have finished, an automated test will:
      1. Read /home/user/db_logs/query_metrics.log and compare it byte-for-byte
         against the required format and values.  
      2. Read /home/user/db_logs/high_latency_queries.sql and verify that
         it contains the correct subset of SQL statements in the correct
         order.

    Tip: you do NOT need to write any explanatory output to the console
         for the tests to pass—only the two result files are checked.
truth:
Initial assets
    --------------
    Directory: /home/user/db_logs (already created, mode 755)

    Slow log file: /home/user/db_logs/slow_query.log  (mode 644)
    Exact contents:

# Time: 2023-10-11T10:15:23
# User@Host: root[root] @ localhost []
# Query_time: 3.125 Lock_time: 0.000 Rows_sent: 12 Rows_examined: 100
SET timestamp=1697024123;
SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = 42;
# Time: 2023-10-11T10:16:00
# User@Host: app[app] @ 192.168.1.10 []
# Query_time: 0.256 Lock_time: 0.001 Rows_sent: 1 Rows_examined: 5
SET timestamp=1697024160;
SELECT name FROM products WHERE price > 1000;
# Time: 2023-10-11T10:17:35
# User@Host: analytics[anl] @ 192.168.1.20 []
# Query_time: 7.890 Lock_time: 0.002 Rows_sent: 25000 Rows_examined: 500000
SET timestamp=1697024255;
SELECT email, created_at FROM audit_logs WHERE created_at < '2023-01-01';
# Time: 2023-10-11T10:18:12
# User@Host: root[root] @ localhost []
# Query_time: 1.050 Lock_time: 0.000 Rows_sent: 0 Rows_examined: 0
SET timestamp=1697024292;
DELETE FROM sessions WHERE last_activity < NOW() - INTERVAL 30 DAY;
# Time: 2023-10-11T10:20:44
# User@Host: api[api] @ 192.168.1.30 []
# Query_time: 12.500 Lock_time: 0.010 Rows_sent: 5 Rows_examined: 1500000
SET timestamp=1697024444;
UPDATE orders SET status='FAILED' WHERE payment_state='error';
# Time: 2023-10-11T10:22:05
# User@Host: app[app] @ 192.168.1.10 []
# Query_time: 0.600 Lock_time: 0.001 Rows_sent: 1 Rows_examined: 10
SET timestamp=1697024525;
SELECT name FROM customers WHERE customer_id = 123;

    Ground-truth calculations
    -------------------------
    Query_time values (s): 3.125, 0.256, 7.890, 1.050, 12.500, 0.600  
    Sum            : 25.421  
    Average (3 dp) : 4.237  
    Maximum (3 dp) : 12.500  
    Count          : 6

    Therefore /home/user/db_logs/query_metrics.log must contain:

Average_Query_Time(s)=4.237
Longest_Query_Time(s)=12.500
Total_Queries=6

    High-latency (Query_time > 5) statements, in order:

SELECT email, created_at FROM audit_logs WHERE created_at < '2023-01-01';
UPDATE orders SET status='FAILED' WHERE payment_state='error';

    So /home/user/db_logs/high_latency_queries.sql must contain exactly
    those two lines in that order, each terminated by a newline, and
    nothing else.
